perturbation.original_text: Kennedy covenants and agrees: (a) For a period of three (3) years commencing on the Closing Date (the “Restricted Period”), Kennedy shall not, nor shall Kennedy permit any of his Affiliates, directly or indirectly, (i) engage in or assist others in engaging in the Autocam Business; (ii) have an interest in any Person that engages directly or indirectly in the Autocam Business in any capacity, including as a partner, shareholder, member, employee, principal, agent, trustee, or consultant; or (iii) intentionally interfere in any material respect with the business relationships (whether formed before or after the date of this Agreement) between the Company and customers or suppliers of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Kennedy covenants and agrees: (a) For a period of three (3) years commencing on the Closing Date (the “Restricted Period”), Kennedy shall endeavor not to, nor shall Kennedy permit any of his Affiliates, directly or indirectly, (i) engage in or assist others in engaging in the Autocam Business; (ii) have a significant interest in any Person that engages directly or indirectly in the Autocam Business in any capacity, including as a partner, shareholder, member, employee, principal, agent, trustee, or consultant; or (iii) interfere in any material respect with the business relationships (whether formed before or after the date of this Agreement) between the Company and customers or suppliers of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the obligation to a mere attempt, and adding 'significant interest' makes the definition of interest ambiguous. Also removing 'intentionally' from the third clause weakens it. This is a non-compete agreement governed under Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be narrowly tailored and unambiguous to be enforceable. The changes introduce ambiguity that undermines enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires that non-compete agreements are reasonable in scope and clearly defined. The use of the phrase 'endeavor not to' introduces ambiguity, making it unclear what level of effort is required of Kennedy to avoid violating the agreement. This ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce, as it's unclear when Kennedy would be in breach. Additionally, replacing the precise term 'have an interest' with the subjective term 'have a significant interest' creates further ambiguity about what constitutes a prohibited interest, which weakens the enforceability of the non-compete clause under Delaware law. The law favors clarity and strict construction in non-compete agreements.

Contracts Subchapter III. Equipment Dealer Contracts Â§ 2720. Definitions. As used in this subchapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) âConstruction,â âfarm,â âindustrialâ and âoutdoor power,â when used to refer to tractors, implements, attachments or repair parts, have the meanings commonly used and understood among dealers and suppliers of those trades. (2) âContract agreementâ means a written or oral contract or agreement between a dealer and a supplier by which the dealer is granted the right to sell their equipment and the dealer is required to order and maintain an inventory in excess of $25,000 at current net price from the supplier. (3) âCurrent net priceâ means the price listed in the supplierâs price list in effect at the time the contract agreement is terminated, less any applicable discount allowed. (4) âDealerâ means a person, firm or corporation engaged in the business of selling, at retail, construction, farm, industrial or outdoor power equipment and who maintains a total inventory of new equipment and repair parts valued at $50,000 or over and provides repair service for the above-mentioned equipment. (5) âInventoryâ means the tractors, implements, attachments, equipment and repair parts that the dealer purchased from the supplier. (6) âNet costâ means the price the dealer paid the supplier for the inventory, less all applicable discounts allowed, plus the amount the dealer paid for freight costs from the supplierâs location to the dealerâs location, plus reasonable cost of assembly performed by the dealer. (7) âSupplierâ means a wholesaler, manufacturer or distributor who enters into a contract agreement with a dealer. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Delaware Code Title 6. Commerce and Trade § 2707 | FindLaw Delaware Code Title 6. Commerce and Trade § 2707. Agreements not to compete Current as of January 01, 2022 | Updated by FindLaw Staff Any covenant not to compete provision of an employment, partnership or corporate agreement between and/or among physicians which restricts the right of a physician to practice medicine in a particular locale and/or for a defined period of time, upon the termination of the principal agreement of which the said provision is a part, shall be void; except that all other provisions of such an agreement shall be enforceable at law, including provisions which require the payment of damages in an amount that is reasonably related to the injury suffered by reason of termination of the principal agreement. Provisions which require the payment of damages upon termination of the principal agreement may include, but not be limited to, damages related to competition. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity in key terms of the non-compete agreement. Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the obligation, and replacing 'have an interest' with 'have a significant interest' makes the scope of prohibited interests unclear. The Law Explanation emphasizes that Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be clearly defined. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Delaware Code Title 6, § 2707, addresses agreements not to compete, suggesting that such agreements are subject to legal scrutiny in Delaware. The changes in the contract introduce ambiguities that weaken the enforceability of the non-compete clause, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (b) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall not permit any of its Affiliates to, directly or indirectly, hire or solicit any employee of the Company or encourage any such employee to leave such employment or hire any such employee who has left such employment, except pursuant to a general solicitation which is not directed specifically to any such employees; provided, that nothing in this Section 1 shall prevent Kennedy or any of his Affiliates from hiring (i) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company before the Merger, (ii) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company after the Merger one-hundred eighty (180) days from the date of termination of employment; or (iii) Jesse Miramontes (a current employee of the Company) and any Kennedy family member (including, without limitation, Nancy Kennedy (wife) or John C. Kennedy, IV (son) who are currently employed by the Company).
perturbation.changed_text: (b) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall use reasonable efforts to prevent any of its Affiliates from, directly or indirectly, hire or solicit any employee of the Company or encourage any such employee to leave such employment or hire any such employee who has left such employment, except pursuant to a general solicitation which is not directed specifically to any such employees; provided, that nothing in this Section 1 shall prevent Kennedy or any of his Affiliates from possibly hiring (i) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company before the Merger, (ii) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company after the Merger one-hundred eighty (180) days from the approximate date of termination of employment; or (iii) Jesse Miramontes (a current employee of the Company) and any Kennedy family member.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not permit' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to prevent' weakens the restriction. The usage of 'possibly hiring' instead of 'hiring' and 'approximate date' introduce ambiguity. Under Delaware law, non-solicitation agreements must be clear and unambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law requires that non-solicitation agreements are reasonable in scope and clearly defined. The changes introduce ambiguity that undermines enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the restriction. 'Reasonable efforts' is a subjective standard and invites dispute about what actions are sufficient to comply. Also 'approximate date' means the 180 day period can be debated. Also adding 'possibly hiring' means they can claim that it wasn't 'hiring' but something else. This contradicts the requirement for clear and definite terms in restrictive covenants under Delaware law. These agreements must be narrowly tailored to protect legitimate business interests and avoid undue hardship.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not permit' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to prevent' weakens the original obligation, introducing ambiguity. Additionally, the terms 'possibly hiring' and 'approximate date' create further ambiguity, especially concerning the 180-day termination period. According to the Law Explanation, this contradicts Delaware law requirements for clear and definite terms in restrictive covenants, which must be narrowly tailored. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Delaware Code Title 6 § 2707, addresses agreements not to compete, reinforcing the need for clarity and enforceability in such agreements. The weakened language and ambiguous terms in the modified text would likely fail legal scrutiny, creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall not permit any of his Affiliates to, directly or indirectly, solicit or entice, or attempt to solicit or entice, any clients or customers of the Company or potential clients or customers of the Company for purposes of diverting their business or services from the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall somewhat discourage his Affiliates to, directly or indirectly, solicit or lightly entice, or remotely attempt to solicit or entice, any clients or customers of the Company or potential clients or customers of the Company for purposes of diverting their business or services from the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not permit' with 'shall somewhat discourage' weakens the obligation, 'lightly entice' and 'remotely attempt' creates ambiguity and uncertainty as to the degree of solicitation or enticement that is prohibited.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law requires that non-solicitation agreements are reasonable in scope and clearly defined. The changes introduce ambiguity that undermines enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes introduce significant ambiguity into the non-solicitation clause. 'Somewhat discourage' is vague and unenforceable compared to 'shall not permit.' It's unclear what actions constitute 'somewhat discouraging' affiliates from soliciting. Replacing 'solicit or entice' with 'solicit or lightly entice' further weakens the provision. Delaware courts require non-compete and non-solicitation agreements to be narrowly tailored and unambiguous to be enforceable. This modification directly contradicts that requirement, making it difficult to determine if Kennedy has breached the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that directly contradicts the requirement for clarity in non-compete and non-solicitation agreements, as indicated by the Law Explanation. The original text used 'shall not permit,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text uses 'shall somewhat discourage,' which is vague and unenforceable. Furthermore, changing 'solicit or entice' to 'solicit or lightly entice' introduces ambiguity, weakening the provision. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Delaware Code Title 6. Commerce and Trade § 2707, addresses agreements not to compete. While it primarily concerns physicians, the Law Explanation extends the principle of narrowly tailored and unambiguous agreements to non-solicitation clauses, suggesting that Delaware law favors clear and definite terms in restrictive covenants. The weakening and ambiguity introduced in the changed text create a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (b) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and interpreted in accordance with the substantive laws of the state of Delaware, without regard to the choice or conflict of law rules of such state.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) This Agreement shall preferably be governed by and construed and interpreted with some consideration for the laws of the state of Delaware, while having regard for the choice or conflict of law rules of another suitable state.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed' to 'shall preferably be governed' weakens the governing law clause. Also changing 'in accordance with' to 'with some consideration for' reduces the certainty of Delaware law being applied. The clause now suggests the possibility of applying conflict of law rules from another state, which is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under Delaware law, choice of law provisions must be clear and unambiguous. The changes introduce uncertainty as to whether Delaware law will actually govern the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing 'shall be governed' to 'shall preferably be governed' and introducing the phrase 'with some consideration for,' the modification weakens the certainty of Delaware law applying. Also adding 'another suitable state' introduces ambiguity as it isn't clear what 'suitable' means. This is a direct contradiction of the principle that choice of law provisions should be clear, unambiguous, and definitive to ensure predictability and enforceability. Under Delaware law, if the intent to apply Delaware law is unclear due to ambiguous language, there is a risk that a court may not enforce the choice of law provision.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be governed' to 'shall preferably be governed' and 'in accordance with' to 'with some consideration for' introduces ambiguity regarding which state's laws will govern the agreement. The addition of 'another suitable state' further weakens the certainty of Delaware law's application. This contradicts the principle that choice of law provisions should be clear and unambiguous to ensure predictability and enforceability. The provided law snippet, while not directly on point, does relate to contracts under Delaware law (Title 6, Chapter 27), and the altered language introduces ambiguity into a core contractual term.
